issue: Stripe webhook returns HTTP 200 on signature verification failure, so a forged event is effectively accepted by the caller and still processed in practice; verification rejection is not signalled with a 4xx and the function does not return early on a real failure path that matters for downstream trust.
evidence: try { event = stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, sig, endpointSecret); } catch (err) { return NextResponse.json({ message: "Webhook error", error: err }); }
recommendation: Return status 400 on constructEvent failure (NextResponse.json({...},{status:400})). A 200 tells Stripe/attacker the request 'succeeded'. Also do not echo the raw error object back in the response body.

issue: createTransaction credits tokens to an arbitrary client-supplied buyerId with no authentication or ownership check. It is exported as a server action and is reachable from the webhook with attacker-controlled metadata.buyerId, enabling credit/token fraud and crediting other users' accounts.
evidence: export async function createTransaction(transaction: CreateTransactionParams) { ... await db.insert(TransactionsTable).values({ plan: transaction.plan, userId: transaction.buyerId }); const response = await updateCredits({ plan: transaction.plan, userId: transaction.buyerId });
recommendation: This action must only run from a verified Stripe webhook. Verify the webhook signature first (see stripe route finding), trust buyerId only from a signed Stripe event, and do not export this as a generally callable server action. Add idempotency keyed on stripeId to prevent replay double-crediting.

issue: updateCredits('use server' exported action) takes a userId parameter and grants tokens with no auth() check and no verification that the caller owns that userId. Any authenticated (or unauthenticated, since there is no auth() call) caller can mint tokens for any user.
evidence: export async function updateCredits({ plan, userId }: { plan: string; userId: string; }) { ... await db.update(UserTable).set({ tokens: Number(user.tokens) + updateTokensValue }).where(eq(UserTable.id, userId)); }
recommendation: Do not expose credit mutation as a callable server action keyed on a client userId. Either gate it behind a verified webhook only, or derive userId from auth() and require server-side proof of payment. Never trust a client-supplied userId for privilege/credit changes (ADR-0001 mandates app-layer authz since RLS is disabled).

issue: getReportById queries ReportsTable by reportId only, with no userId/ownership filter and no auth() call. Any caller can read any user's AI report (IDOR). RLS is disabled per ADR-0001, so the missing app-layer filter is the only control and it is absent.
evidence: const report = await db.query.ReportsTable.findFirst({ where: eq(ReportsTable.id, reportId) }); return { success: true, report: report?.reportData as ReportType };
recommendation: Call auth(), then constrain the query: where: and(eq(ReportsTable.id, reportId), eq(ReportsTable.userId, userId)). Return 404/null when not owned.

issue: saveReport accepts userId as a function parameter with no auth() check and inserts a report under that arbitrary userId. A caller can write reports into another user's account / spoof ownership.
evidence: export async function saveReport(report: ReportType, userId: string) { const result = await db.insert(ReportsTable).values({ userId, reportData: report })
recommendation: Derive userId from await auth() inside the action; never accept it from the client. Reject when unauthenticated.

issue: CLERK_WEBHOOK_SKIP_VERIFY env flag fully bypasses Svix signature verification, parsing the raw body as a trusted event. If this env is ever set in a deployed/staging environment an attacker can forge user.created events and create arbitrary DB users.
evidence: const SKIP_VERIFY = process.env.CLERK_WEBHOOK_SKIP_VERIFY === "1"; ... if (SKIP_VERIFY && !svixId && !svixTs && !svixSig) { evt = JSON.parse(body); }
recommendation: Hard-gate the bypass to non-production only (refuse if NODE_ENV === 'production'), or remove it entirely and use a local Svix test secret instead. A verification bypass switch is a dangerous footgun.

issue: Hardcoded fallback API key 'local-m3-change-me' is used as the valid auth key whenever NODE_ENV !== 'production'. This well-known constant (also shipped in .env.example and multiple MCP files) effectively makes the flag-mutation endpoint open to anyone who reads the source in any non-production deploy.
evidence: const DEV_API_KEY = "local-m3-change-me"; function getValidApiKey() { if (process.env.FEATURE_FLAGS_API_KEY) return process.env.FEATURE_FLAGS_API_KEY; if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production") return DEV_API_KEY; ... }
recommendation: Remove the hardcoded fallback. Require FEATURE_FLAGS_API_KEY in all environments and fail closed if unset. Rotate the value, and never document a real key value in .env.example (use a placeholder).

issue: Same hardcoded shared secret 'local-m3-change-me' is the default for BOTH the bearer token (M3_MCP_API_KEY) and the upstream app key (FEATURE_FLAGS_API_KEY). Combined with the server binding to HOST 0.0.0.0 by default, an unconfigured deployment exposes flag mutation to the network with a publicly-known credential.
evidence: const HOST = process.env.M3_HTTP_HOST ?? "0.0.0.0"; const DEV_API_KEY = "local-m3-change-me"; const BEARER = process.env.M3_MCP_API_KEY ?? DEV_API_KEY; const APP_API_KEY = process.env.FEATURE_FLAGS_API_KEY ?? DEV_API_KEY;
recommendation: Require M3_MCP_API_KEY and FEATURE_FLAGS_API_KEY explicitly (fail closed if missing). Default HOST to 127.0.0.1 instead of 0.0.0.0 so the wrapper is not network-exposed by default.

issue: API key comparison uses non-constant-time '===' string equality, which is theoretically vulnerable to timing analysis. Same pattern in the MCP HTTP wrapper bearer check.
recommendation: Compare secrets with crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length buffers (hash both sides first to normalise length) to avoid leaking key bytes via response timing.

issue: The webhook logs ALL request headers (including the svix-signature header, a security token) and later logs user PII (clerkId, email, name) to stdout. This persists sensitive signing material and PII in application logs.
evidence: req.headers.forEach((value, key) => { allHeaders[key] = value; }); console.log("[clerk-webhook] All headers:", allHeaders); ... console.log("[clerk-webhook] Creating user:", { clerkId, email, name });
owasp: A09 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
recommendation: Remove header dumps and PII from logs. Log only non-sensitive correlation IDs (svix-id) at debug level, and never log signature headers or email addresses in production.

issue: Raw, attacker-influenced LLM output and full request error messages are logged to stdout, and raw error.message is returned to the client. Verbose internal error strings (DB/Anthropic errors) leak implementation details to callers.
evidence: console.log("Raw Claude response:", responseText); ... errorMessage = error.message; return new Response(JSON.stringify({ error: errorMessage }), { status: 500 ... })
recommendation: Return a generic error message to clients and log details server-side only. Avoid logging full model outputs that may contain user trade data (PII). Same issue exists in follow-up-claude/route.ts:84.

issue: Token decrement is non-atomic (read-then-write race). Two concurrent requests both read the same token count and each decrement independently, allowing a user to exceed their token allotment. The check 'user?.tokens === 0' and the subsequent set are separated by an await with no transaction or atomic SQL decrement.
evidence: const updatedTokens = user.tokens - 1; await db.update(UserTable).set({ tokens: updatedTokens }).where(eq(UserTable.id, user.id))
recommendation: Use an atomic conditional update: UPDATE users SET tokens = tokens - 1 WHERE id = ? AND tokens > 0 (Drizzle sql`tokens - 1`), and treat 0 rows affected as 'insufficient tokens'. Applies to follow-up-claude/route.ts:43 too.

issue: No request-size limit or rate-limiting on the authenticated Claude endpoints. The full 'trades' payload is JSON.stringified and forwarded to the paid Anthropic API, enabling cost-amplification / DoS by a single authenticated user (one token spends up to 4000 output tokens of paid model time on attacker-sized input).
evidence: const data = await request.json(); const { trades } = data; ... text: JSON.stringify(trades)
recommendation: Add per-user rate limiting and a max body/trade-count cap, and validate the trades payload with Zod before forwarding to Anthropic. Same for follow-up-claude.

issue: uploadScreenshot/deleteScreenshot interpolate a caller-supplied 'key' directly into the R2 object URL with no validation, allowing path components ('../', leading '/', absolute URLs) to point writes/deletes at unintended object keys if any caller passes user-influenced input. contentType is also taken verbatim with no allowlist.
evidence: const url = `${endpoint}/${bucket}/${key}`; const res = await r2.fetch(url, { method: "PUT", body: body as BodyInit, headers: { "Content-Type": contentType } });
recommendation: Server-generate keys (e.g. `${userId}/${uuid}.png`) or strictly validate key against ^[A-Za-z0-9/_-]+\.(png|jpe?g|webp)$, reject '..' and leading '/', and allowlist contentType. Also enforce a max body size (ADR-0002 notes the whole file is buffered in memory).

issue: Plan-to-token mapping is inconsistent and not derived from the actual Stripe amount. stripe.ts maps {'10':20,'5':60} while user.ts maps {'5':20,'10':60}, and webhook trusts metadata.credits/plan rather than the verified amount_total. This data-integrity gap lets the wrong (or attacker-chosen, via forged metadata) credit amount be granted.
evidence: stripe.ts: const PLAN_TOKEN_MAP = { "10": 20, "5": 60 }; vs user.ts: const PLAN_TOKEN_MAP = { "5": 20, "10": 60 };
owasp: A08 Software and Data Integrity Failures
recommendation: Derive credits server-side from the verified Stripe line items / amount_total (and a single canonical price->credits table), not from client/webhook metadata. Reconcile the two divergent maps into one shared constant.

issue: Errors are swallowed and logged with console.log(err) across multiple actions, and several actions return generic {error:true} which is acceptable, but stack/PII may end up in logs. Minor logging hygiene / inconsistent error handling.
owasp: A09 Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
recommendation: Use a structured logger at error level (not console.log), avoid dumping raw error objects that can contain query parameters/PII, and add audit logging for security-relevant mutations (credit changes, deletes).

issue: Feedback message is stored with no length cap and no rate limiting; only trim() and non-empty are checked. An authenticated user can submit arbitrarily large messages (storage/DoS) and there is no abuse throttling.
evidence: if (!message || message.trim().length === 0) { ... } await db.insert(FeedbackTable).values({ userId, message: message.trim() });
recommendation: Validate with Zod (e.g. max 5000 chars) and add per-user rate limiting on feedback submission.

issue: Dependency CVE scanning (npm audit) was intentionally skipped per caller constraints (offline environment). Known-vulnerable transitive dependencies may exist and were not assessed.
evidence: npm audit not run (offline). Stack: Next.js, Drizzle, @anthropic-ai/sdk, stripe, svix, aws4fetch, @qdrant/js-client-rest, langchain, @xenova/transformers.
recommendation: Run `pnpm audit --prod` (or npm audit) in an online CI step and track results; pin and update flagged packages, especially auth/crypto-adjacent ones (svix, stripe).

note: Per-user ownership filters are correctly applied in journal.ts, trades.ts (update/delete), strategies.ts (delete/edit), and archive.ts addRemoveFavorite/deleteReportFromDB/getReports. These follow ADR-0001 app-layer authz correctly; only getReportById and saveReport/createTransaction/updateCredits are broken.
